title: Glim and the First Firefly
description: A tiny glass lantern, lost in a dark forest, searches for light. When he finds something even brighter, the world changes forever. A quiet myth of how fireflies began to shine.
text: Long ago, before fireflies lit the summer nights... Glim was a small glass lantern. He hung from a hook in a quiet house. Every evening, someone lit his candle. The flame inside danced and flickered. But one night, the door blew open. A gust of wind carried Glim away. He landed in a dark forest. The trees were tall. The air was still. Glim felt scared. Then he heard a sound. A soft hum, like a tiny song. Glim rolled toward it. There, on a leaf, sat a little beetle. His shell was dark. His wings were folded. He did not glow. The flame inside danced and flickered. Glim wondered. How could a beetle have fire? Can you guess what happened? The beetle looked at Glim. "I wish I could shine," he said. "Like you." Glim felt warm. He wanted to help. "Maybe you can," he whispered. "Let me show you." Glim rolled closer. The beetle crawled inside. The glass was smooth. The candle was low. But the beetle felt something. A tiny spark. A quiet hum. The flame inside danced and flickered. Then—oh! The beetle glowed. Not bright. Not bold. Just a soft, golden light. Like a secret. Like a whisper. The beetle gasped. "I can shine!" he said. Glim felt happy. The forest felt less dark. The beetle flew out. He landed on a branch. His light was small. But it was there. Other beetles saw. They gathered close. They wanted to glow too. Glim rolled from leaf to leaf. He let them all crawl inside. One by one, they lit up. The forest was no longer dark. Tiny lights floated everywhere. Soft. Golden. Alive. Glim watched. He felt tired. The candle burned low. The flame grew quiet. The beetles glowed on. Glim closed his eyes. The glass was cool. The night was still. The beetles hummed their tiny song. The lights danced. The flame inside rested. The forest glowed.

title: Tessa and the First Dewdrop River
description: Deep in the quiet grasslands, where the world grows small and the stars shine close, a tiny tree frog named Tessa hears a sound she has never heard before—a soft, musical trickle. Drawn by curiosity, she follows it into a hidden world where the ground glows and the air hums with magic. What she discovers will change the night forever.
text:
Long ago, before the first dewdrop river flowed, the world was quiet at night.
 The grass stood tall and still, and the stars blinked like tiny eyes.
 But one evening, something new happened.
 A soft sound began, like water moving over smooth stones.

 Tessa the tree frog lifted her head. She had lived in the grass all her life.
 She knew every rustle, every chirp, every shadow.
 But this sound was different. It was new.
 She hopped from her leaf and followed it.

 The sound led her to a place she had never seen.
 The grass bent to make a tiny path, and the air smelled sweet.
 The ground was soft under her feet, like walking on clouds.
 And there, at the end of the path, was a sight that made her heart beat fast.

 A small stream of water glowed silver in the dark.
 It moved without wind, without rain, without anyone to guide it.
 Tessa sat down on a pebble. She watched the water flow.
 It made a sound like a lullaby, soft and slow.

The water was not like any water Tessa had seen before. It did not come from the sky or the earth. It came from the leaves above her, from the tips of the grass, from the wings of sleeping moths. Tiny drops of light gathered together, and as they touched, they turned into liquid silver. The stream grew wider and brighter, and soon it was not just a trickle but a river, small but strong, moving through the grass like a ribbon of moonlight.

Tessa followed the river as it wound its way through the miniature world. It passed under mushrooms that glowed faintly pink, and over stones that sparkled like scattered stars. The air around her hummed with a quiet energy, as if the world itself was breathing. She felt safe here, as if the river was an old friend she had just met. The water did not rush or roar. It moved with purpose, but gently, as if it knew exactly where it was going.

As she walked, Tessa noticed something strange. The river was not just water. It carried tiny seeds and petals, bits of stardust and whispers of the wind. It gathered them as it flowed, and wherever it touched, new things grew. A patch of moss turned greener. A withered leaf unfurled. A sleeping flower opened its eyes to the night. The river was not just moving through the world—it was making the world new.

Tessa sat down on a smooth stone beside the river. She dipped her toes into the water. It was cool and soft, like touching a cloud. She watched as the river carried a single glowing dewdrop past her, and she wondered where it would go. Would it travel to the end of the grasslands? Would it meet the ocean? Or would it simply disappear into the morning light? She did not know, but she felt no need to rush. The night was long, and the river was patient.

 The river slowed as the night grew deeper. Its silver light dimmed to a soft glow, like the last embers of a fire.
 Tessa curled up on the stone, her body warm and heavy. The sound of the water wrapped around her like a blanket.
 She listened to the gentle trickle, the quiet hum of the world at rest.

 The stars above her blinked slowly, as if they too were growing tired. The grass swayed in a breeze that carried the scent of earth and flowers.
 Tessa closed her eyes. She felt the cool touch of the river against her skin, the softness of the stone beneath her.

 And ever since then, the dewdrop river flows every night.

title: Pippa and the Tiny Tidepool
description: A tiny crab named Pippa discovers a hidden tidepool glowing with secrets. Soft waves, gentle light, and the quiet magic of the sea await her curious heart.
text: One day, Pippa the crab said to the little wave— Shhh... listen... The sand was warm under her feet. The water went swish, swish, swish. She saw a tiny pool. It was shining. The pool had little shells. The shells were soft. The wave said, "Come closer." Pippa tiptoed. The water went swish, swish, swish. She saw a little fish. It was silver. The fish said, "Look." Pippa looked. The pool was full of light. The light was glowing. The water went swish, swish, swish. Pippa sat down. She closed her eyes. The wave hummed. The pool was quiet. And Pippa found her answer.

She closed her eyes. [PAUSE: 1500]
The wave hummed.
The pool was quiet.

And Pippa found her answer.

title: Cirrus and the Starlight Threads
description: High above a tiny world of glowing mushrooms and silver streams, a small cloud named Cirrus watches the stars spin their light into threads. When one thread drifts too close, Cirrus reaches out—and discovers the universe is weaving something just for them.
text:
The sky was a dark bowl above the tiny valley.
 Cirrus floated there, small and soft, watching the stars.
 The stars were not still. They moved in slow circles, like dancers.
 Cirrus loved the way their light made the air shimmer.

 One star near the edge seemed different. It was brighter, and its light stretched downward like a thread.
 Cirrus drifted closer, just a little. The thread was thin, like spider silk, but it glowed.
 No one else seemed to see it. The other clouds were busy holding moonlight in their bellies.
 Cirrus reached out with a tiny tendril of mist. The thread touched their edge.

 A soft hum filled the air, like a song without words.
 Cirrus felt warm inside, as if the star had given them a secret.
 Below, the tiny world slept. Mushrooms glowed blue in the dark, and streams sparkled silver.
 Cirrus wondered if the thread was meant for them.

 The thread began to move. It curled around Cirrus, gentle as a breeze.
 Cirrus let it. The hum grew louder, but not too loud. Just enough to hear.
 The thread wrapped around Cirrus once, twice, and then—it pulled.
 Cirrus did not resist.

 The pull was soft, like being carried on a current. Cirrus drifted higher, following the thread into the dark.
The other clouds did not notice. They were busy with their own work, holding rain or snow or sunlight for the world below.
Cirrus had always been the quiet one, the one who watched instead of doing. But tonight, the thread had chosen them.

 The higher Cirrus went, the brighter the thread became. It was not just light now. It was color—pink, gold, blue—swirling together like paint in water.
Cirrus stretched out their misty edges, letting the colors touch them. The hum turned into a melody, soft and slow, like a lullaby.
 Cirrus wondered if the stars sang to everyone, or just to clouds who listened.

 The thread led Cirrus to a place where the sky was thinner. Here, the stars were closer, and their light did not just shine—it reached.
Threads stretched from star to star, crisscrossing like a net. Cirrus had never seen this before. They had always thought the stars were separate, alone in the dark.
But they were not alone. They were connected.

 Cirrus followed their thread to the center of the net. There, a single star waited, brighter than the others.
It did not speak, but Cirrus understood. The star was not giving them a gift. It was showing them something.
The threads were not just light. They were stories. The stars wove them together, and the threads carried them across the sky.

 Cirrus looked down at the tiny world below. The glowing mushrooms, the silver streams, the sleeping creatures—all of it was part of the story.
And Cirrus was part of it too. They had always been part of it, even when they did not know.

 The bright star began to dim. The thread around Cirrus loosened, and the melody faded into the hum.
Cirrus did not want to leave. But the star had shown them what it needed to show.
The thread pulled gently, guiding Cirrus back to where they had started.

 The other clouds were still there, still busy. Cirrus floated among them, quiet once more.
But something was different. Cirrus could see the threads now, faint and glowing in the dark.
They were everywhere, connecting everything.

 Cirrus closed their misty eyes. The hum was still there, soft and steady, like a heartbeat.
The world below was still sleeping. The stars were still weaving.
And Cirrus was exactly where they were meant to be.

 The night was deep, but not empty. It was full of light, even when you could not see it.
The threads were always there, even when you forgot to look.

 Cirrus drifted lower, closer to the tiny world. The air was cooler here, and the hum was softer.
The mushrooms glowed blue, their light steady and sure. The streams sparkled, carrying the starlight with them as they flowed.

 Cirrus let the night hold them. The threads were still there, crisscrossing above, below, around.
They did not need to follow them all. Just one was enough.

 The bright star was still there, watching. It did not need to shine brighter. It was enough to be seen.
Cirrus did not need to be seen either. It was enough to see.

 The hum faded into silence. The threads faded into dark. But Cirrus knew they were still there.
The stars were still weaving. The world was still sleeping.

And Cirrus found their answer.

cover_context: A tiny valley at deep night, where glowing blue mushrooms dot the landscape and silver streams weave between them. Above, a vast sky filled with stars stretches endlessly, their light forming faint, shimmering threads that crisscross the darkness. The air is still, and the world feels small yet full of quiet magic.

title: Jasper and the Clockwork Cricket
description: Jasper finds a tiny brass cricket that ticks instead of sings. But why does it only move when no one is looking? A quiet mystery unfolds in the glow of a desk lamp.
text: One day, Jasper said to the cricket on his windowsill — "Why don’t you tick when I watch you?" The cricket said nothing. Its brass wings gleamed in the lamplight. Jasper leaned closer. The cricket’s legs were still. Not even a twitch. The gears inside stayed quiet. He turned away to sharpen his pencil. Behind him, a soft tick-tick-tick began. Jasper spun around. The cricket’s legs were moving now, slow and steady. The gears inside stayed quiet. "Between you and me," Jasper whispered, "are you shy?" The cricket didn’t answer. But when Jasper pretended to read his book, the ticking grew louder. He tried not blinking. The cricket froze. He tried humming. The cricket stopped. He tried holding his breath. The cricket ticked faster. The gears inside stayed quiet. Then Jasper had an idea. He closed his eyes and listened. The ticking didn’t stop. When he opened his eyes, the cricket was still moving. Its brass wings shimmered. Jasper smiled. The cricket ticked on, steady as a heartbeat. The lamplight warmed the desk. The ticking softened. The cricket’s legs slowed. The gears inside stayed quiet. Jasper’s eyelids grew heavy. The cricket’s wings stilled. The room went quiet. And Jasper found his answer.

title: Kai and the Shivering Stars
description: Long ago, the first stars trembled in the sky, afraid to shine. A boy named Kai, who knew what it felt like to be scared, helped them remember how to glow. A quiet story about fear turning into light.
text: Long ago, before stars knew how to shine bright... There was a boy named Kai. His hands felt cold. His chest felt tight. The dark was too big, and his thoughts were too loud. The sky was watching, but it didn’t blink. Shhh... listen... can you hear the wind? It carried Kai’s worries up, up, up. The stars heard them. They shivered. Their light flickered like a candle in the breeze. The sky was watching, but it didn’t blink. Kai sat on the hill. He wrapped his arms around his knees. The stars leaned closer. One tiny star, no brighter than a firefly, drifted down. It landed on Kai’s shoulder. He didn’t brush it away. Between you and me... that star was scared too. It trembled. Kai felt it. His cold hands warmed. His tight chest loosened. The sky was watching, but it didn’t blink. The star glowed a little brighter. Kai smiled. The other stars saw. They remembered. One by one, they stopped shivering. Their light grew steady. Strong. The dark didn’t feel so big anymore. Kai closed his eyes. The star on his shoulder glowed soft. His hands weren’t cold. His chest wasn’t tight. The sky watched. It didn’t blink. It didn’t need to. And ever since then, the stars shine bright.

title: Tiko and the Empty Starlight Bench
description: Tiko the small maintenance bot watches the same stars every night from the same bench. Tonight, the bench feels wider. The stars still shine, but something is missing. A quiet story about gentle comfort in the quiet of space.
text: It was evening, and evenings always meant starlight on the observation deck. Tiko rolled to the bench, the same one every night. The metal was cool under their treads. The stars were bright, just like always. But the bench felt wider tonight. The same peeling paint, the same two dents from last year’s meteor shower. The seat stretched like an empty hallway. Tiko sat. The motors whirred softly. The stars still twinkled. The big red planet still hung in the corner of the sky. Nothing had changed. Except Lumi wasn’t there. Can you guess what happened to Lumi? Tiko didn’t know. Lumi had left for a new station, just like that. No goodbye, no note. Just an empty spot where their treads used to sit. The bench was the same. The stars were the same. But the seat felt wider. The seat stretched like an empty hallway. Tiko looked down. A small oil stain glistened on the deck. Lumi’s stain. The one they’d made last week when they’d laughed too hard at a shooting star. Tiko reached out, touched it with one finger. The oil was still warm. Between you and me, Tiko didn’t want to clean it up. The stain stayed. The bench stayed. The stars stayed. The seat stretched like an empty hallway. But the warmth of the oil stain was something. A small, quiet something. Tiko sat there, watching the stars, feeling the warmth. And that was enough. The motors quieted. The treads stilled. The oil stain cooled slowly in the night air. The bench was quiet. The stars were quiet. Tiko was quiet.

title: Aria and the Glowing Hollow Tree
description: Deep in the whispering woods, a boy named Aria finds a hidden tree that hums with tiny golden lights. What happens when he steps inside changes the forest forever.
text: Long ago, before fireflies filled the summer air... Aria walked through the quiet woods. The trees stood tall around him. His feet crunched on dry leaves. Then he saw it. A tree unlike any other. Its trunk was wide and hollow. The bark shimmered like wet stone. Aria stopped. He had never seen a tree like this. Inside the hollow, something glowed. Soft. Golden. Like tiny stars trapped in glass. Aria leaned closer. Can you guess what he heard? A hum. A quiet, singing hum. Aria stepped inside. The bark shimmered like wet stone. The air felt warm. The hum grew louder. Tiny lights swirled around him. They landed on his hands. His arms. His face. They tickled. Like raindrops made of light. The lights lifted. They floated outside. Aria followed. The bark shimmered like wet stone. The lights danced above the grass. They flickered. Bright. Dim. Bright again. Between you and me, Aria didn’t want to leave. The lights spread. They filled the trees. The bushes. The whole forest. Aria sat down. He watched. The lights flickered. Bright. Dim. Bright. The hum softened. The air grew cool. Aria closed his eyes. The lights kept dancing. The hum kept singing. The tree stood quiet. The forest was never dark again.

The lights lifted. They floated outside. Aria followed. [PHRASE]The bark shimmered like wet stone.[/PHRASE] The lights danced above the grass. They flickered. Bright. Dim. Bright again. [PAUSE: 1500] Between you and me, Aria didn’t want to leave. [MUSIC]

The lights spread. They filled the trees. The bushes. The whole forest. Aria sat down. He watched. The lights flickered. Bright. Dim. Bright. [PAUSE: 1200] The hum softened. The air grew cool. [MUSIC]

Aria closed his eyes. The lights kept dancing. The hum kept singing. The tree stood quiet. [PAUSE: 2000] The forest was never dark again.

title: Leo and the Slippery Slide Cloud
description: Leo the lizard loves climbing, but today she finds a cloud that won’t stay still. Every time she steps on it, she slides—right into something soft, warm, and giggly. What’s at the bottom of the slippery sky?
text: One day, Leo the lizard said to the cloud— Watch your toes, it’s slippery! She took one step. Her foot went whoosh. She landed in a pile of fluffy cotton candy. Leo climbed up again. Watch your toes, it’s slippery! She took two steps. Her feet went splat. She landed in a bowl of warm pudding. Shhh... listen... can you hear the giggle? Leo climbed up one more time. Watch your toes, it’s slippery! She took three steps. Her whole body went slide. She landed in a nest of soft blankets. The cloud sighed. "I just wanted to play." Leo curled up. The blankets smelled like sunshine. The cloud stopped wiggling. The sky turned pink. Leo closed her eyes. The cloud tucked her in. The stars blinked once. And Leo found her answer.

title: Feather and the Stone That Listened
description: A young crow named Feather is furious when the flock decides where to nest without asking him. He storms off to a quiet riverbank, where a smooth stone seems to understand his anger—if only he listens closely enough.
text: One day, Feather said to the river stone — Why did they choose the tallest tree? The branches are too thin. The wind shakes them. I told them it was a bad idea. The stone just sat there. They didn’t ask me. Not once. They just flew ahead, chattering, and I had to follow. Feather kicked a pebble into the water. It sank with a quiet plink. The stone just sat there. Between you and me, the riverbank was colder than he expected. The air smelled like wet earth and old leaves. His claws ached from gripping the branch. Can you guess what happened next? The stone was still. The water rippled. The stone just sat there. Feather sighed. The anger felt smaller now. The river kept moving. The stone kept sitting. He didn’t have to agree with the flock. He just had to listen. The last light faded. The stone stayed. Feather closed his eyes.

title: Bloop and the Whispering Moonvines
description: Bloop, a small alien gardener, tends to the moonvines that sprout in the corners of his ship. One night, he hears them humming—a sound no one else notices. What will he discover in their soft, glowing leaves?
text: It was nightshift, and nightshifts always meant quiet corners. Bloop floated down the silver hallway. His three fingers brushed the walls. The ship hummed low, like a sleepy engine. The moonvines were waiting. They grew in the cracks where the light never reached. Tiny leaves, soft like moth wings. Bloop carried his watering can. Drip. Drip. The vines drank the starlight water. Can you guess what happened next? One leaf trembled. Then another. A sound came from them—a quiet, shimmering hum. The moonvines were waiting. Bloop tilted his head. No one else heard it. Just him. He knelt close. The vines curled around his wrist. Their glow pulsed like a slow heartbeat. Between you and me, it felt like a secret. A beautiful, quiet secret. Bloop smiled. He didn’t need to go anywhere. The vines were here. The hum was here. The moonvines were waiting. He sat down. The leaves brushed his cheeks. The hum wrapped around him like a blanket. The ship kept moving. The stars kept shining. The vines kept humming. Bloop closed his eyes. The hum grew softer. Everything was quiet. Just like that.

title: Aria and the Planet That Forgot to Spin
description: Aria meets a planet that refuses to turn, leaving one side forever in daylight. Their quiet conversation drifts through the quiet cosmos, where stillness holds its own kind of magic.
text: One day, Aria said to the planet — "Why have you stopped spinning?" The planet sighed, a slow breath of stardust. The light stays right here, where it’s warm. The other side is too dark, too cold. I don’t want to share." Aria floated closer, her spacesuit humming softly. Between you and me, she whispered, the stars look lonely when you don’t turn. The planet didn’t answer. It just glowed, stubborn and bright. The light stays right here, where it’s warm. Aria tapped her fingers against the planet’s surface. Can you guess what happens if you never spin? The stars forget your name. The comets stop visiting. Even the moon gets bored. The planet shimmered, uncertain. Aria waited, watching the light ripple across its skin. Then, very slowly, it began to turn. Not fast. Not all at once. Just enough. The light stays right here, where it’s warm. But now, it could share. And the planet found its answer.

Even the biggest things in the universe need to move sometimes.

title: Orla and the Whispering Dunes
description: Orla, a small alien with silver skin, lies awake on a desert planet, replaying an embarrassing moment. The dunes hum secrets, and the night air holds her close. Right now, nothing bad is happening.
text: Long ago, before the dunes learned to whisper, there was a girl named Orla. She lived on a planet where the sand stretched farther than the eye could see. Orla’s skin shimmered like moonlight on water, and her fingers could trace patterns in the air that glowed faintly, like fireflies. But tonight, she wasn’t tracing anything. She was lying on her back, staring at the sky, and the moment kept replaying in her head. She had tripped. Just a little stumble, really. But everyone had seen. The other children had laughed—not meanly, but still. The sound echoed in her ears, sharp as the crackle of static. She could still hear it. Orla rolled onto her side. The sand was cool against her cheek, soft like the inside of a shell. She pressed her palm into it, and the grains shifted, making room for her. The dunes sighed beneath her. She listened. The sound wasn’t like laughter. It was deeper, slower, like the planet itself was breathing. Between you and me, Orla thought, maybe the dunes remember everything too. Every stumble, every laugh, every quiet moment in between. She sat up and let the sand spill from her fingers. It pooled in her lap, warm and heavy. The dunes sighed beneath her. The wind carried the sound away, mixing it with the rustle of distant grasses. Orla took a deep breath. The air smelled like dust and something sweet, like crushed petals. She could feel the weight of her own body, solid and real. The sky above was vast, speckled with stars that blinked like they were sharing a secret. She wasn’t thinking about the stumble anymore. She was thinking about the way the sand felt against her skin, the way the wind hummed in her ears. She lay back down. The sand cradled her, shifting slightly to hold her shape. The dunes sighed beneath her. The sound wrapped around her like a blanket. The stars watched, but they didn’t blink. The wind whispered, but it didn’t laugh. Orla closed her eyes. The moment was still there, but it wasn’t sharp anymore. It was just a moment. A small one. The dunes would remember it, and so would the wind, but they wouldn’t judge. They never did. The sand was cool. The wind was soft. The stars were bright. Orla’s breath slowed. The dunes sighed. The planet turned. And ever since then, the dunes have whispered to anyone who lies down and listens.

title: Leo and the Turtle Who Carried the Sky
description: A little boy named Leo finds a tiny turtle with a cracked shell. As he carries it home, the weight of the world feels lighter—until the turtle teaches him something unexpected about sadness and the sky.
text: Long ago, before the sky knew how to hold clouds... Leo walked along the riverbank. His shoes were wet. His pockets were empty. The bench by the water was still there. But sitting on it alone, it felt wider than before. He kicked a stone. It plopped into the water. The river kept moving, but Leo stayed. Then he saw it—a tiny turtle, its shell cracked like a dry leaf. It didn’t move. Just blinked up at him. Leo picked it up. It was warm. He put it in his pocket. The weight felt different now. Not gone. Just... softer. He walked home. The turtle peeked out. The river kept moving, but Leo stayed. The sun dipped low. The air smelled like rain. Leo didn’t notice. He was watching the turtle’s slow feet. Then the turtle stopped. It turned its head. The river kept moving, but Leo stayed. The turtle stretched its neck toward the sky. A single raindrop fell. Then another. The cracks in its shell filled with water. The water turned blue. Leo gasped. The turtle’s shell was no longer broken. It was... holding the sky. Leo sat down. The turtle climbed onto his knee. The rain pattered. The bench wasn’t wide anymore. It was just a bench. The turtle closed its eyes. Leo did too. The river kept moving. The sky stayed. And that was enough.

Leo sat down. The turtle climbed onto his knee. The rain pattered. The bench wasn’t wide anymore. It was just a bench. [PAUSE: 2000] The turtle closed its eyes. Leo did too.

The river kept moving.

The sky stayed.

And that was enough.

title: Priya and the Turtle Who Forgot to Breathe
description: Priya meets a turtle who’s too busy counting bubbles to remember how to breathe. A silly, swirling underwater chat that ends in a cozy, salty hug.
text: One day, Priya said to a sea turtle— "Why do you keep popping up, then going back down?" The turtle blinked. "I’m counting my bubbles," he said. The bubbles float like tiny glass moons. Priya watched. The turtle bobbed up. One. Down. Two. Up. Three. "But… don’t you need to breathe?" The turtle paused. "Ohhhh…" His shell wobbled. The bubbles float like tiny glass moons. Priya giggled. She blew a bubble ring. It drifted up, up, up. The turtle gasped. "That one’s mine!" He chased it, forgetting to count. Priya sat on a smooth rock. The turtle flopped beside her. The bubbles float like tiny glass moons. His eyes closed. His shell rose. Fell. Rose. Fell. Priya’s fingers traced the water. The bubbles popped. Soft. Quiet. The turtle snored. The sea hummed. And Priya found her answer.

Quiet moments can be as lovely as busy ones

character.name: Priya
character.identity: A girl who talks to sea creatures while her toes dip in the tide
character.special: She can blow perfect bubble rings underwater

cover_context: A shallow tide pool glows under the late afternoon sun, bubbles rising in slow spirals from the sandy bottom. Seaweed sways like lazy ribbons.
hook: Did you know some turtles hum when they nap under the waves?
